title: Variables
description: What a variable is, declaring vs initializing, naming rules, reassignment, and using variables with cout/cin — formalizing the int age = 25; preview from earlier lessons.
objectives: Understand what a variable is and why programs need them
Declare and initialize a variable, and tell the two apart
Follow C++'s variable naming rules and common conventions
Reassign a variable's value after it's declared
Use a variable together with cout and cin

sections.text: 1. What is a Variable?
Back in the Program Structure lesson, we previewed int age = 25; without explaining it. Today we explain it properly.
A variable is a named storage location that holds a value a program can use, change, and refer to by name.

sections.title: The labeled locker analogy
sections.text: Think of a variable like a labeled locker: the label (name) stays the same, but what's inside (the value) can be swapped out. age is the label; 25 is what's currently inside.
2. Declaring a Variable
This declares a variable named age that can hold a value of type int (a whole number). At this point, age exists, but we haven't given it a value yet.
Declaring a variable means telling the compiler its name and type, reserving a place to store a value of that type.
3. Initializing a Variable
This declares age and gives it a starting value in the same line — that's called initialization.

rows: int age; — reserves the variable, no value yet
int age = 25; — reserves the variable and gives it a starting value

sections.text: Reading an uninitialized variable's value before assigning one (e.g. printing age right after int age; with no = 25) gives an unpredictable, meaningless value — often called a garbage value. Always initialize a variable before reading it.
4. Assigning a New Value
Once declared, a variable's value can be changed by assigning a new one — no int keyword this time, since it's already declared.
This is the age = age + 1; line previewed earlier. It reads age's current value, adds 1, and stores the result back into age — a very common pattern for updating a value based on itself.
5. Naming Rules for Variables

sections.title: A variable name in C++
points: Must start with a letter or an underscore (_) — not a digit
Can contain letters, digits, and underscores after that
Cannot contain spaces or symbols like -, @, or #
Cannot be a reserved C++ keyword (like int, return, or main)
Is case-sensitive — age and Age are different names

sections.code:
int age;      // valid
int _score;   // valid
int total1;   // valid

int 1total;   // invalid — starts with a digit
int my age;   // invalid — contains a space

sections.text: Recall from Program Structure: C++ is case-sensitive, which is why Main and main were different names. The same rule applies to every identifier, including variable names.
6. Naming Conventions
Beyond what the compiler requires, good variable names are a habit worth building early.

sections.title: Common conventions
points: Use meaningful names — age, not x, when the value represents someone's age
camelCase is common in C++: totalMarks, studentName
Avoid single letters except for very short-lived, obvious cases (loop counters, covered later)

sections.text: 7. Multiple Variables in One Line
Several variables of the same type can be declared — and optionally initialized — in a single statement, separated by commas.
8. Variables Need a Type
int is just one type — it means a whole number. Every variable in C++ must have a type, which determines what kind of value it can hold.
We have not covered C++'s other data types yet (like decimals or text) — that's the next topic. For now, we're only using int so the idea of a variable is clear.
9. Where Are Variables Declared?
So far, we've only declared variables inside main()'s braces { }, which is where our program's statements live.

A variable generally must be declared before it's used — the compiler reads top to bottom, so cout << age; needs age to already exist above it. Where exactly a variable can be seen and used from is called its scope, which we'll study properly in a dedicated topic.
10. Using a Variable with cout and cin

sections.title: Tracing it
points: age is declared and initialized to 25
Second cout prints Age after birthday: 26

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Using a variable before declaring it — the compiler reads top to bottom and won't recognize a name it hasn't seen yet.
Reading a variable's value before initializing it — this gives an unpredictable garbage value, not 0 or an error.
Redeclaring a variable with int again after it already exists in the same scope — int age; ... int age = 5; in the same block is invalid.
Starting a name with a digit, or using spaces/symbols — 1total and my age are invalid names.
Forgetting variable names are case-sensitive — age and Age are two different variables, not the same one.
Forgetting the semicolon after a declaration — int age = 25 (missing ;) is a compilation error, same rule as any statement.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: What is a variable?
questions.answer: A variable is a named storage location that holds a value a program can use, change, and refer to by name.

questions.question: What is the difference between declaring and initializing a variable?
questions.answer: Declaring reserves a variable with a name and type but no value yet (int age;), while initializing gives it a starting value at the same time (int age = 25;).

questions.question: What happens if you read an uninitialized variable?
questions.answer: It contains a garbage value — unpredictable leftover data, not a guaranteed default like 0 — so it should always be initialized before being read.

questions.question: What are the rules for naming a variable in C++?
questions.answer: A name must start with a letter or underscore, can contain letters/digits/underscores after that, cannot contain spaces or most symbols, cannot be a reserved keyword, and is case-sensitive.

questions.question: Can multiple variables be declared in one statement?
questions.answer: Yes — for example, int a, b, c; or int a = 1, b = 2;, separating each with a comma.

questions.question: Why does a variable need a type?
questions.answer: The type tells the compiler what kind of value the variable can hold and how much memory to reserve for it.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: A variable is a named storage location that holds a value a program can use and change. Declaring a variable, like int age;, reserves it with a name and type but no value; initializing it, like int age = 25;, gives it a starting value right away. Variable names must start with a letter or underscore, can't contain spaces, can't be reserved keywords, and are case-sensitive. Once declared, a variable's value can be reassigned, like age = age + 1;, and variables are commonly used together with cout to display values and cin to read new ones from the user.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Variables
sections.text: Variable = Labeled locker. Declare = put the label on an empty locker. Initialize = put something inside right away. Assign = swap what's inside later.
